questions.question: What is an array in C?
options: A collection of elements of different types stored in contiguous memory
A collection of elements of the same type stored in contiguous memory locations
A chain of nodes each containing data and a pointer to the next
A composite type that groups variables of different types under one name

questions.question: What formula calculates the memory address of an element in an array?
options: base_address + index
base_address × (index + size_of_data_type)
base_address + (index × size_of_data_type)
index × size_of_data_type

questions.question: Array indexing in C and Python begins at:
options: Depends on the compiler

questions.question: What does each node in a linked list contain?
options: Only data
Only a pointer to the next node
A piece of data and a pointer to the next node
An index and a value

questions.question: What is the main memory advantage of arrays over linked lists?
options: Arrays use less memory because they do not store pointers
Arrays allow dynamic resizing at runtime
Arrays have no fixed size limit
Arrays require traversal before access

questions.question: What is the main advantage of linked lists over arrays for insertions?
options: Linked lists use less total memory
Only two pointers need to be adjusted; no elements need to be shifted
Linked lists support instant random access
Linked lists allocate memory at compile time

questions.question: Which is a disadvantage of linked lists compared to arrays?
options: Fixed size after declaration
Cannot store the same data type in consecutive nodes
No random access — elements must be traversed sequentially from the head
Elements are stored in contiguous memory

questions.question: A struct in C is best described as:
options: A collection of elements of the same type in contiguous memory
A composite data type that groups variables of different types under a single name
A pointer to a dynamically allocated memory block
A LIFO structure used for function call management

questions.question: How are struct fields laid out in memory?
options: In alphabetical order by field name
In reverse order of declaration
Sequentially in the order they are declared
Interleaved with heap allocations

questions.question: What is memory padding in a struct?
options: Extra bytes reserved for future fields
Empty bytes inserted between fields to satisfy memory alignment requirements
Bytes used to store the struct's type information at runtime
Unused stack space reserved by the OS

questions.question: What is a pointer in C?
options: A variable that stores a copy of another variable's value
A variable that stores the memory address of another variable
A reference to a function's return value only
A data type specifically for floating point numbers

questions.question: Which operator accesses a struct member through a pointer in C?
Given: int number = 10; int *ptr = &number; — what does ptr contain?
options: The value 10
The value 0
The memory address of number
A copy of the variable number

questions.question: The base address of an array is:
options: The address of the last element
The address of the middle element
The memory address of the first element
The total size of the array in bytes

questions.question: Inserting an element into the middle of an array is inefficient because:
options: The array must be reallocated on the heap
All subsequent elements must be shifted to make room
The array's type must be re-verified by the compiler
Pointers to each element must individually be updated

questions.question: LIFO stands for:
options: Last In, First Out
Last In, First Over
Linked In, First Out
Load In, Free Out

questions.question: Which operation adds an item to the top of the stack?
options: Pop
Peek
Push
Insert

questions.question: Which operation removes an item from the top of the stack?
options: Push
Pop
Dequeue
Shift

questions.question: In a downward-growing x86 stack, a push operation:
options: Increments ESP then stores the value at the new address
Decrements ESP then stores the value at the new address
Stores the value then increments ESP
Stores the value then decrements ESP

questions.question: Which x86 register tracks the top of the stack?
options: EAX
EBP
ESP
EIP

questions.question: Which x86 register tracks the base of the current stack frame?
options: ESP
EBP
EAX
EIP

questions.question: A stack frame contains which of the following?
options: Only local variables
Only the return address
Local variables, return address, saved registers, and parameters
Heap allocations and global variables

questions.question: What is the return address stored in a stack frame?
options: The starting address of the callee function
The address of the next instruction to execute after the function returns
The address of the function's first local variable
The current value stored in EBP

questions.question: Why is EBP used alongside ESP in a stack frame?
options: EBP stores the return value while ESP stores parameters
ESP can change during function execution; EBP provides a stable reference point for the frame
EBP holds global variables while ESP holds local variables
Both registers serve identical purposes

questions.question: After a pop operation, the data that was at the old stack top:
options: Is permanently erased from memory immediately
Remains in memory but will be overwritten on the next push
Is automatically moved to the heap
Is copied into EAX by the CPU

questions.question: When a function exits, what happens to its local variables on the stack?
options: They are copied to the heap for later use
They are preserved in a CPU register
They are discarded as the stack frame is removed
They remain on the stack until the program ends

questions.question: Function arguments are pushed onto the stack in what order?
options: Left to right (first argument first)
In alphabetical order by parameter name
Reverse order (last argument first)
Random order determined by the compiler

questions.question: The primary purpose of the call stack is:
options: To allocate dynamic heap memory
To store global and static variables
To manage execution context and track return addresses for function calls
To cache frequently accessed data for the CPU

questions.question: Values 1, 2, 3 are pushed (in that order) onto an empty stack. After one pop, what is now on top?
options: The stack is empty

questions.question: Saved registers in a stack frame allow the callee to:
options: Use those registers freely, then restore them before returning so the caller's data is preserved
Pass extra arguments to nested function calls
Monitor register usage for the operating system
Access heap allocations through the frame pointer

questions.question: What is the first action a caller takes before making a function call?
options: Push the return address directly
Save registers that need to be restored after the call
Allocate space on the heap for the callee
Set EBP to the callee's first instruction address

questions.question: How does the caller transfer control to the callee function?
options: By copying EBP into ESP
By setting the Program Counter to the first instruction of the callee
By pushing the callee's local variables onto the stack
By calling malloc() to allocate a stack frame

questions.question: What is the first thing the callee saves when setting up its stack frame?
options: Its return value in EAX
The old frame pointer — the caller's EBP
Its local variable values
The current stack pointer value in a register

questions.question: Where is a function's return value typically placed in x86?
options: On the heap
In EBP
In EAX
At the top of the stack after the frame is cleaned up

questions.question: What is pass by value?
options: The memory address of the argument is pushed onto the stack
A copy of the argument is pushed onto the stack; changes inside the function do not affect the original
The argument is stored in a global variable accessible by the callee
The argument is always passed through a CPU register

questions.question: What is pass by reference?
options: A full copy of the argument is passed to the function
The memory address of the argument is passed; changes inside the function affect the original variable
The argument is stored as an immutable local variable
The argument's type descriptor is passed rather than its value

questions.question: Why is passing a large struct by reference more efficient than pass by value?
options: References are stored on the heap which is faster to access
Only the memory address (~4 bytes) is pushed onto the stack rather than copying the entire struct
Pass by reference bypasses the stack entirely
Large structs cannot be passed by value in C

questions.question: In C, the call findLargest(&myStruct) passes the struct:
options: By value — a full copy is made on the stack
By reference — only the memory address is passed
By pointer to a heap allocation
By bytecode reference as in Java

questions.question: When a function returns, the Program Counter is set to:
options: The address of the callee's first instruction
The return address stored in the stack frame
The current value of ESP
Zero to signal completion

questions.question: After a function returns, the frame pointer (EBP) is restored to:
options: The callee's own base pointer
The caller's saved base pointer that was stored in the stack frame
The current value of the stack pointer
The return address value

questions.question: How does an OOP class primarily differ from a C struct?
options: Structs support inheritance; classes do not
Classes add methods, constructors, and inheritance; structs only contain data fields
Classes store data in contiguous memory; structs scatter data across the heap
Structs and classes are functionally identical in modern C

questions.question: What does typedef struct { float x; float y; float z; } Point; accomplish?
options: Declares an array of three floats named Point
Defines a struct and creates the alias Point so it can be used directly as a type without the struct keyword
Creates a class with three getter methods
Declares three separate global float variables

questions.question: What does p->x do when p is a pointer to a struct?
options: Dereferences p and copies x into a general-purpose register
Accesses the field x of the struct that p points to
Increments p by the size of x in bytes
Declares a new local variable x within the struct's scope

questions.question: Modularity in software design:
options: Is only achievable through Object-Oriented Programming
Can be achieved in C using structs and separate functions even without OOP
Requires classes and inheritance to implement properly
Is incompatible with the C programming language

questions.question: After stack frame cleanup on a function return, the stack pointer (ESP) is set to:
options: The callee's base pointer address
Zero to reset the stack
The top of the heap
The entry point address of main()

questions.question: Which C function allocates a block of memory on the heap at runtime?
options: alloc()
new()
malloc()
create()

questions.question: Which C function releases previously allocated heap memory?
options: delete()
free()
release()
dealloc()

questions.question: What is the key difference between stack allocation and heap allocation?
options: Stack is allocated manually by the programmer; heap is allocated automatically by the CPU
Stack allocation happens automatically when a function's stack frame is created; heap allocation requires an explicit runtime request (e.g. malloc)
Stack allocation happens at compile time before the program runs; heap allocation happens at runtime
Stack and heap both require explicit allocation and deallocation by the programmer

questions.question: What is the lifetime of heap-allocated memory?
options: It ends when the function that allocated it returns
It ends when explicitly freed by the programmer or when the program terminates
It ends after a fixed timeout managed by the OS
It ends when the pointer referencing it goes out of scope

questions.question: The heap is the appropriate memory region when:
options: Data is only needed for the duration of a single function call
Memory size is not known at compile time or data must persist beyond a single function call
All data elements are of the same type
The program uses less than 1MB total memory

questions.question: What is heap memory fragmentation?
options: When malloc() exceeds the system's total RAM
When repeated allocation and deallocation leaves scattered free blocks that are difficult to reuse as a single large block
When the heap grows downward and collides with the stack
When pointer arithmetic causes a misaligned memory access

questions.question: Given: int *p = malloc(sizeof(int)); — where is each part stored?
options: Both the pointer p and the allocated block are on the heap
p is in a CPU register; the block is on the stack
The pointer variable p is on the stack; the allocated memory block is on the heap
Both the pointer and the block are on the stack

questions.question: What is the first stage of the C build process?
options: Compilation
Linking
Preprocessing
Assembling

questions.question: What does the C preprocessor do?
options: Converts C code to machine code directly
Expands macros, #include files, removes comments, and handles conditional compilation
Resolves all external function references
Converts assembly language to object code

questions.question: What file extension does the assembler produce?
options: .c
.s
.i
.o

questions.question: What is the role of the linker in the C build process?
options: Converts C source to assembly language
Expands all #include directives
Resolves all function references and combines object files into a final executable
Loads the finished executable into RAM

questions.question: At the object code (.o) stage, calls to library functions such as printf() are:
options: Already fully resolved and executable
Not yet resolved — they will be resolved later by the linker
Removed by the preprocessor before this stage
Converted directly into system calls by the assembler

questions.question: What does the Program Loader do?
options: Compiles C source code to machine code
Links object files together into an executable
Reads the executable from disk and places it into RAM for execution
Converts assembly language instructions to object code

questions.question: Java source code is compiled to what intermediate form?
options: Native machine code for the host CPU
AT&T syntax assembly language
Bytecode that is executed by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM)
Preprocessed C code

questions.question: Which execution model provides the fastest runtime performance?
options: Java bytecode interpreted by the JVM
Python scripts interpreted line-by-line
C source compiled directly to native machine code
JavaScript interpreted by a browser engine

questions.question: Match each C build stage to its correct file extension — Source → Preprocessed → Assembly → Object → Executable:
options: .c → .o → .s → .i → .exe
.c → .i → .s → .o → executable
.i → .c → .s → .o → executable
.c → .s → .i → .o → executable

questions.question: Given: int *p = malloc(sizeof(int)); declared inside a function — where does the variable p itself live?
options: On the heap, because it is involved in a malloc call
In a CPU register permanently
On the stack, as a local variable of the function
In the .data section of the executable

questions.question: Given: int *p = malloc(sizeof(int)); — where does the allocated memory block live?
options: On the stack alongside p
On the heap at the address returned by malloc
In the preprocessor output (.i file)
In a CPU register until the function returns

questions.question: Given: int *p = malloc(sizeof(int)); and p stores address 0x5000 — which statement is correct?
options: The variable p lives at 0x5000 on the heap
The variable p is on the stack; the integer-sized block is at heap address 0x5000
Both p and the block are at heap address 0x5000
Both p and the block are on the stack

questions.question: A function declares: char *buf = malloc(100); — when the function returns without calling free(buf), what happens?
options: The OS automatically reclaims the heap block because the pointer went out of scope
The stack frame is cleaned up and p is gone, but the 100-byte heap block is leaked — still allocated with no pointer to it
The heap block is moved onto the stack so it survives the return
The compiler inserts a free() automatically at the end of the function

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between a pointer variable and the memory it points to?
options: The pointer variable and the pointed-to memory always occupy the same memory region
The pointer variable stores an address; the memory at that address is a separate block that may be in a completely different region (e.g., pointer on stack, block on heap)
Pointer variables can only point to other stack variables
Pointer variables are stored on the heap by default

questions.question: int *p = malloc(sizeof(int)); *p = 42; — what does *p = 42 do?
options: Changes the address stored in p to 42
Writes the value 42 into the heap-allocated block that p points to
Declares a new integer variable on the stack with value 42
Frees the heap block and replaces it with the value 42

questions.question: Why does a local pointer variable disappear after its function returns, even when the heap block it pointed to is still allocated?
options: The heap block is automatically freed when any pointer to it goes out of scope
The pointer is a local variable on the stack frame, which is removed on return; the heap block has no automatic lifetime — it persists until freed
The CPU overwrites heap memory when a stack frame is popped
Local variables are moved to the .data section when a function exits

questions.question: What controls the lifetime of a stack-allocated local variable?
options: The programmer must explicitly free it with free()
The OS tracks it and reclaims it after a timeout
Its lifetime is tied to the function call — it is created when the frame is pushed and destroyed when the frame is popped
It persists for the entire duration of the program

questions.question: What controls the lifetime of heap-allocated memory?
options: The function call that allocated it — it is freed automatically when that function returns
The programmer — it persists until explicitly freed with free(), regardless of which function allocated it
The compiler — it inserts free() calls at the end of each scope
The OS — it reclaims heap memory when the allocating stack frame is popped

questions.question: Stack allocation is described as 'automatic' because:
options: The programmer writes alloc() and the compiler converts it to stack instructions
The OS pre-allocates all stack memory before the program starts
Memory is reserved and released automatically as functions are called and return — no explicit programmer action required
The CPU randomly selects which variables go on the stack

questions.question: Which scenario requires heap allocation rather than stack allocation?
options: A loop counter used only within a single function
A function parameter passed by value
A large data structure that must remain accessible after the function that created it has returned
A temporary integer used in an arithmetic calculation

questions.question: What is the decimal value 12 expressed in hexadecimal?
options: 0xA
0xB
0xC
0xD

questions.question: What is the hexadecimal value 0x10 in decimal?
What is 0x1000 + 12 expressed in hexadecimal?
Given: int arr[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50}; with base address 0x1000. What is the address of arr[0]?
Given: int arr[5] with base address 0x1000 and sizeof(int) = 4. What is the address of arr[3]?
options: 0x1003 (forgot ×4)
0x1008 (index 2)
0x100C (correct)
0x1010 (index 4)

questions.question: Given: int arr[5] with base address 0x1000 and sizeof(int) = 4. What is the address of arr[4]?
Given: int arr[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50} with base address 0x1000 and sizeof(int) = 4. What value is stored at address 0x1008?
For int arr[5], what happens in C when you attempt to access arr[10]?
options: The program automatically returns 0
A compile-time error stops execution
Undefined behavior — C performs no bounds checking; the program may crash, corrupt data, or expose a security vulnerability
The element is automatically initialized to 0

questions.question: Given: struct Person { int id; char name[4]; double salary; }; starting at address 0x4000. What is the address of the name field?
Given: struct Person { int id; char name[4]; double salary; }; starting at address 0x4000. What is the address of the salary field?
What is the total size in bytes of: struct Person { int id; char name[4]; double salary; }? (int=4B, char=1B, double=8B)
As a general rule, which field ordering minimizes wasted padding bytes in a struct?
options: Alphabetical order by field name
Smallest fields first, largest last
Largest fields first, smallest last
Order does not matter — the compiler always optimizes layout

questions.question: Linked list: nodes c→b→a exist. A new node d (value 40) is inserted between c and b. What must d.next point to after the insertion?
options: NULL
c
b
a

questions.question: How many pointer assignments are required to insert a new node into the middle of a linked list?
In: void example() { int x = 10; int* y = (int*)malloc(sizeof(int)); *y = 20; free(y); } — where does the variable x live?
options: Heap
Stack
CPU register
.data section of the executable

questions.question: In the same example() function — where does the pointer variable y live?
options: Heap, because it is used with malloc
Stack, as a local variable of example()
CPU register permanently
The same memory location as *y

questions.question: In the same example() function — where does *y (the integer value 20) live?
options: Stack
CPU register
Heap, allocated by malloc
.data section
The program crashes immediately on return
A compile-time error is produced
A memory leak — the heap block is still allocated but the pointer is gone and the memory can never be freed
The OS reclaims the heap block automatically when the pointer goes out of scope

questions.question:
Calculate the memory address (express with 0x prefix, e.g. 0x1004):

int arr[5]; // base address = 0x1000, sizeof(int) = 4

What is the address of arr[2]?

Calculate the memory address (express with 0x prefix, e.g. 0x1004):

int arr[5]; // base address = 0x1000, sizeof(int) = 4

What is the address of arr[3]?
Express the decimal value 12 as a hexadecimal number with a 0x prefix (e.g. 0xA):
